steps.title: The Mask Unmade

activity.name: Musée des Explorations du Monde — Interior Investigation & Final Cipher Resolution
activity.address: Place de la Castre, Le Suquet, 06400 Cannes

steps.narrative_intro:
The heavy wooden door closes behind you and the noise of Le Suquet — the gulls, the distant hum of the port — cuts to silence. The air inside the Musée des Explorations du Monde smells of old stone and polished wood, centuries folded into glass cases. You're standing in the castle that once watched over every ship entering Cannes harbor, built by monks from the same Lérins monastery that produced the reliquary that started all of this.

Your phone vibrates. Three messages arrive simultaneously — Vera, Noor, and Yael, all aware you've entered the museum. Each wants something different from the next few minutes. The cipher's final coordinate points to an object in the Mediterranean antiquities collection on the ground floor. A specific vessel. A specific inscription. The map was always leading here — to the place where the jailer hid something inside the one institution nobody would ever think to rob: a museum that didn't exist yet.

But Vera's cipher is a forgery. You know that now. Which means the coordinates she gave you point to something she planted in the narrative — not something the jailer hid. The real question isn't what the cipher says. It's what's actually here, in this collection, that Vera is so desperate for you to find. And why Le Pharmacien just sent you a photograph of a page from the prison registry of Fort Royal, dated 1698, with one entry circled: an inventory of items transferred with the masked prisoner. Item 7: "un vase de terre, contenant des papiers, scellé."

A sealed clay vessel containing papers. Transferred with the prisoner. Lost when Napoleon dismantled the fortress archives.

You're looking for a Provençal clay vessel in the Mediterranean antiquities collection. If it's here — if it was always here, miscatalogued, hiding in plain sight for three centuries — then Vera didn't forge the trail. She reverse-engineered it from real evidence. And the documents inside may have been read by the museum's curators without anyone understanding what they had.

The yacht in the Vieux Port isn't going anywhere. Yael's people have delayed its departure with a customs inspection. You have time. But Marco just went silent, and Noor's last message ended mid-sentence. Something has shifted. Find the vessel. Photograph it. And decide who gets to know what you find.
steps.instruction: Your ticket has been activated through the app. Enter the Musée des Explorations du Monde and make your way to the Mediterranean antiquities collection on the ground floor. You are looking for a Provençal clay vessel — likely terracotta, likely 17th century or catalogued as such — among the Mediterranean artifacts. Examine the vessels on display. Look for any that match the description: sealed or once-sealed, Provençal origin, roughly the size described in a prison transfer inventory. Photograph the vessel or vessels you believe could be the one referenced in the 1698 Fort Royal registry. Then climb the Tour du Suquet — the medieval watchtower — and from the panoramic viewing platform, photograph the Vieux Port below. Noor needs visual confirmation of the yacht's position, and Vera needs to know if a second vessel has appeared at the eastern dock. Send both photographs through the app. Your choices in the next few minutes determine who walks away with the evidence — and who walks away with nothing.
steps.player_action: Find and photograph a Provençal terracotta vessel in the Mediterranean antiquities collection that could match the 1698 Fort Royal prison transfer inventory description. Then climb the Tour du Suquet watchtower and photograph the panoramic view of the Vieux Port, identifying the yacht's position at the docks below.

gps_trigger.content_brief: A high-resolution scan of a page from the 1698 Fort Royal prison transfer registry, with Item 7 circled in red: 'un vase de terre, contenant des papiers, scellé' — a sealed clay vessel containing papers. Marginal note in different handwriting reads: 'envoyé à la tour de Cannes, inventaire civil.' A second document accompanies it: a partial 19th-century museum acquisition log from the Château de la Castre showing 'divers objets de terre, provenance monastique, Lérins' — miscellaneous clay objects of monastic provenance from Lérins. Le Pharmacien's only annotation: 'It was never lost. It was filed wrong. Room 2, west wall. Go.'

steps.camera_prompt: The player photographs terracotta or clay vessels in the Mediterranean antiquities collection. AI identifies the vessel type, approximate period, provenance information on museum labels, any visible seals or markings. The AI weaves what it sees into the narrative — matching or contrasting the vessel with the 1698 description. If the player photographs the panoramic view from the Tour du Suquet, AI identifies the Vieux Port, dock positions, and any large vessels visible, interpreting them as the seized yacht in the narrative context.

tension.description: All threads converge inside the museum. Vera's forgery is exposed but her trail proved real. Noor has gone silent — possibly intercepted by the buyer's network. Marco's betrayal or loyalty depends on what the player shared with him. Yael is demanding the player transmit everything to her as legal evidence. Le Pharmacien wants public disclosure. The player holds the pivot: who they send the photographs to first determines who controls the discovery. The vessel may actually contain — or have once contained — documents that identify the Man in the Iron Mask. Three centuries of mystery, sitting in a glass case labeled 'Provençal, 17th c., monastic origin, use unknown.'
tension.resolution: The player photographs the vessel and the view from the tower, then chooses who receives the evidence. The twist lands: Vera forged the cipher map, yes — but she forged it by working BACKWARD from real evidence she found in this museum five years ago. She was the anonymous researcher who requested access to the Lérins acquisition archive in 2021. The vessel was opened by conservators in 1987 and the papers inside were catalogued as 'liturgical fragments, untranslated.' They are still in the museum's archive. Vera has been engineering this entire day to force an official re-examination of those papers — because no one believed her when she published her findings. The reliquary, the auction, the cipher — all theater to create a public event dramatic enough that the documents couldn't be ignored again. The player can validate her scheme or expose it. Either way, the documents get a second look.

character_interactions.trigger: Player sends photograph of the terracotta vessel from the Mediterranean collection
character_interactions.phone_version:
Vera's response arrives after a long pause — longer than any of her previous messages. When it comes, her tone has shifted entirely. The performance is over.

'You found it. I know you found it because I found it five years ago. Same room, same case, same awful fluorescent lighting. I requested access to the Lérins acquisition archive in 2021 under a false academic credential. The conservators opened that vessel in 1987. The papers inside were catalogued as liturgical fragments — untranslated, unstudied, forgotten. I read them. They are not liturgical. They are personal letters, written in a cipher that matches the one used by Saint-Mars, the jailer of the Iron Mask. I published my findings in the Revue d'Histoire Moderne. No one read it. No one cared. So I built all of this — the reliquary, the auction, the cipher map, your involvement — to create a situation so dramatic that the academic world and the press would have to re-examine those papers. You were never authenticating a reliquary. You were authenticating a story. And now you have to decide: do you tell Yael everything, and I go to prison for fraud and forgery? Or do you validate the trail, the documents get examined properly, and the Man in the Iron Mask finally gets a name? I won't insult you by saying there's an easy answer. There isn't. But you've earned the right to make this call. Not Noor. Not Yael. You.'

rayban_version.script: Vera's voice comes through raw, stripped of its usual precision. 'You found it. Same room where I found it five years ago. I published what those papers contain. No one listened. So I fabricated a spectacle — the reliquary, the cipher, you — to force the world to look. Those papers are real. My methods are not. Now you decide who I am: a criminal, or the woman who solved history's greatest mystery and couldn't get anyone to care. Send the photo to Yael and I'm done. Send it to Le Pharmacien and the papers get their day. Your call. I'm tired of making it for everyone.'

contextual_music.track_type: minimal piano, single repeated note dissolving into silence

character_interactions.trigger: Player sends photograph of the Vieux Port panorama from the Tour du Suquet
character_interactions.phone_version:
Yael's message is clipped, professional, but there's an undercurrent of urgency she's no longer hiding.

'Good. I can see the yacht from your angle — eastern berth, third slip. My team boarded twenty minutes ago under customs authority. The skeleton crew had already begun destroying digital records. We recovered partial files linking three shell companies to properties purchased with proceeds from the 2013 Carlton heist. This is the break the investigating magistrate has needed for eleven years. But I need more. If what you're telling me about Vera Solari is accurate — that she engineered a fraudulent auction to manipulate an investigation — that's a separate criminal matter. I need your testimony. I need the photographs. I need the vessel provenance. I understand she may have done this for reasons you find sympathetic. I find them sympathetic too. But I don't get to choose which laws apply based on how I feel about the person breaking them. Neither do you. Send me everything you have, and I will ensure the museum documents are examined through proper forensic channels. Or don't, and watch this become another conspiracy theory that lives and dies on Reddit. Your move, authenticator.'

rayban_version.script: Yael's voice is steady, deliberate — a captain who has waited years for this. 'I can see the yacht from your photo. My team is aboard. We've recovered partial Carlton heist files. Now I need you. If Vera engineered a fraud, I need your evidence. I know she may be right about those documents. But the law doesn't bend for good intentions. Send me what you have. I'll make sure the museum papers get a proper forensic examination. Or keep it to yourself and watch this disappear into myth. You've been an authenticator all day. Authenticate this: does the truth need permission to come out?'

camera_mode.purpose: AI analyzes the panoramic view to identify the yacht position and dock layout, feeding details into Yael's tactical response

character_interactions.trigger: 90 seconds after the Vera interaction resolves
character_interactions.phone_version:
Noor's message arrives fragmented, as though typed in a hurry.

'Still here. Sorry for going dark — the buyer's people found my real name. Had to switch devices. Listen: I don't care about Vera's scheme or Yael's case. My father spent thirty years arguing that the Iron Mask was an Armenian diplomat named Davtyan, and the Sorbonne laughed him out of his career. He died believing he was right. If those papers in the museum prove him wrong, fine. If they prove him right, I need to be the one to publish. Not Vera. Not some government press office. Not a podcast. Me. His daughter. In a peer-reviewed journal with his name as co-author. That's all I've wanted since I walked into that auction. You don't owe me anything. But if you photograph the papers' reference number from the museum catalogue — just the accession number — I can file an official academic access request tonight. Vera gets her vindication. Yael gets her evidence. I get to finish my father's work. This is the only version where everyone wins. Please.'

rayban_version.script: Noor's voice is breathless, hurried. 'I had to switch devices — the buyer's people found me. Listen. I don't care about Vera or Yael. My father died trying to prove the Iron Mask was an Armenian diplomat. If those papers prove anything, I need to publish it. His name, a real journal. Just photograph the accession number from the museum catalogue. I can file an academic access request tonight. Everyone gets what they want. This is the version where nobody loses. Please.'

steps.unlock_message: The door opens onto centuries of collected expeditions — masks, instruments, weapons, maps. Somewhere in this castle, a clay vessel holds the answer to a question that has haunted historians since 1698. Le Pharmacien's documents have just appeared in your vault. Room 2. West wall. The jailer's secret has been hiding in plain sight. Find it.

She implies someone powerful had it removed. The player's first task is to photograph the auction house facade and send it to Vera, who cross-references architectural details with a forged provenance document she unlocks in the vault. Marco independently messages the player, warning them not to trust 'the Italian woman.' The player is caught between two competing versions of reality before they've even taken fifty steps.

The player can challenge her on this or let it pass. If challenged, she deflects with charm but her subsequent messages become more guarded. If the player tells Vera or Morin, Noor's behavior shifts dramatically — she becomes colder, more transactional, and begins feeding the player half-truths designed to lead them away from the real trail.

narrative_beats.description: MORIN'S ULTIMATUM — Capitaine Morin contacts the player directly with an ultimatum: she has enough evidence to arrest Vera Solari within the hour. She wants the player to confirm or deny a single detail — the location of a second forged document. The player must choose: protect Vera (lie or stay silent), help Morin (share what they know), or try to play both sides. This is the moral fulcrum of the quest. Morin is not bluffing — she has real jurisdiction, and her patience has limits. But she's also withholding her own secret: she's been investigating Le Pharmacien for three years and Vera is bait, not the target.
possible_triggers: player arrives at Step 4 GPS zone
player shares incriminating info about Vera with Morin earlier
timer trigger: if player hasn't interacted with Morin by mid-Step 4

narrative_beats.description: DIMITRI'S VANISHING ACT — Dimitri, who has been a background presence providing dry logistical intel and sardonic commentary, suddenly goes silent. His last message is a half-finished sentence: 'The mask isn't what you think it—'. Then nothing. Other characters react differently: Vera panics, Marco says 'he was always going to run,' Noor seems unsurprised, Morin demands to know what the player's last exchange with Dimitri contained. Le Pharmacien sends a single word: 'Dommage.' The player must piece together what happened from the reactions.
possible_triggers: player enters Step 5 GPS zone
player shares critical info with Dimitri that triggers his disappearance
Dimitri's trust reaches a threshold where he attempts to warn the player

narrative_beats.description: THE TWIST DETONATES — The player discovers (through cross-referencing documents, character contradictions, and the final location's real architectural details) that Vera Solari is Le Pharmacien. She has been both the client hiring the player AND the ghost manipulating the game from the shadows. The 'Le Pharmacien' messages were automated by Vera, timed to GPS triggers she set up before the quest began. Her goal was never to find the artifact — it was to use the player's investigation to flush out who in her network betrayed her to Morin. The artifact was already in her possession the entire time. The salt references were her signature: she's a restorer who uses salt-based techniques, and 'ashes' refers to what she plans to do with the forged documents once she identifies the traitor.
possible_triggers: player arrives at Step 6 and photographs the final location
player confronts Vera with accumulated contradictions
Morin reveals her long-term investigation target
player pieces together Le Pharmacien's message timestamps matching Vera's silences

narrative_beats.description: ARG BLEEDTHROUGH — Between Steps 3 and 5, the player receives a communication OUTSIDE the app (with prior consent): a fake email from 'Direction Régionale des Affaires Culturelles — PACA' with an official-looking PDF referencing the player by their chosen alias, listing them as a 'personne d'intérêt' in an ongoing investigation into art trafficking on the Côte d'Azur. The email includes a real DRAC logo and references real French cultural heritage law (Code du Patrimoine, Article L114-1). The player can show this to characters in-app for varied reactions — Morin will confirm it's 'partly real,' Noor will laugh nervously, Marco will say 'welcome to my life.'
possible_triggers: player consented to ARG bleedthrough at quest start
player completes Step 3
timer: 10 minutes after Step 3 completion

resolution_principles: If player trust with Vera is HIGH and trust with Morin is LOW → Vera reveals herself as Le Pharmacien directly to the player in a moment of calculated vulnerability, framing it as a confession between equals. She offers the player a choice: walk away with what they know, or help her finish identifying the traitor. The tone is intimate, conspiratorial, almost tender.

If player trust with Vera is LOW and trust with Morin is HIGH → Morin is the one who unmasks Vera as Le Pharmacien, presenting intercepted message logs that show Vera's dual identity. The revelation feels like a police briefing — clinical, triumphant, and slightly cold. Morin offers the player credit for the investigation.

If the player betrayed Vera to Morin at any point (shared incriminating info during Morin's Ultimatum beat) → Vera's final message is laced with ice: she knew the player would fold, she planned for it, and the player was never her first choice — just the most convenient. Le Pharmacien sends one last message: 'You were the control group.' The player realizes they were tested and found predictable.

If the player protected Vera from Morin AND earned Noor's trust → Noor privately reveals she always suspected Vera's dual identity and was running her own counter-investigation for an unnamed Beirut collector. The ending gains a layer: the player helped one schemer while being watched by another. Noor's final message: 'You chose well. Or you chose badly. In this business, it's the same thing.'

If the player maintained HIGH trust with Marco throughout → Marco sends a final voice memo, slightly drunk, admitting he forged the provenance document not for money but because he genuinely believed the artifact belonged in a museum, not a private vault. His confession recontextualizes him from mercenary forger to idealist who lost his way.

If Dimitri's trust was HIGH before his disappearance → his final half-message is completed post-twist via a delayed timed message revealing he figured out Vera was Le Pharmacien and tried to warn the player. He is safe — he ran because Vera realized he knew.

If the player ignored or antagonized Le Pharmacien's messages throughout → the twist lands harder because Vera reveals she was testing whether the player would engage with the unknown. Dismissing Le Pharmacien means the player failed Vera's real test — but Vera respects the survival instinct.

If the player tried to play ALL sides (shared info freely with everyone) → mutual exposure: every character knows what the player told the others. The twist still lands but the aftermath is chaos — Morin is furious, Marco feels betrayed, Noor vanishes. The player gets the truth but loses every alliance.

Vera Solari.low: Becomes formal, transactional, and slightly condescending. Withholds key documents from the vault. Gives the player only the minimum information needed to keep them moving. Her messages shorten. She stops using the player's name and reverts to 'you.' Drops hints that she has other operatives who could replace the player. Le Pharmacien's messages become MORE frequent — because Vera is channeling her energy into the ghost persona instead.
Vera Solari.medium: Cooperative but guarded. Shares documents but redacts key sections. Engages in intellectual banter — tests the player's knowledge of art history, provenance law, Cannes geography. Flashes of warmth followed by sudden distance. Asks the player probing personal questions then deflects when asked the same. Her messages have a rhythm: three warm ones, then one that's pure ice. She's calibrating.
Vera Solari.high: Drops the professional mask. Messages become longer, more personal. She shares a story about her father — a restorer in Genoa who taught her that 'salt preserves and salt destroys, and the art is knowing which you're doing.' She begins signing messages with her initials instead of her full name. Sends the player an unredacted document that contradicts something she said earlier — a deliberate act of trust, testing if the player will use it against her. If the player is perceptive, her Le Pharmacien messages start containing echoes of Vera's phrasing — the seams of her dual identity begin to show.

Marco Delvecchio.low: Deflects everything with sarcasm and art-world gossip. Actively misleads — sends the player to examine wrong details, gives false historical context, attributes quotes to the wrong people. When caught in a lie, laughs it off: 'In my profession, accuracy is a suggestion.' Blames Vera for everything. Refuses to discuss his own forgery past. Messages are short, flippant, designed to waste the player's time while appearing helpful.
Marco Delvecchio.medium: Genuinely helpful but self-serving. Provides accurate art historical context because he can't resist showing off his expertise. Corrects the player's observations with withering precision but follows up with useful insight. Admits to 'knowing people who forge things' without admitting he's one of them. Begins referencing his own past in oblique terms: 'I once held a Caravaggio that didn't exist yet.' Will cooperate against Vera if the player pushes, but hedges everything.
Marco Delvecchio.high: Becomes almost paternal. Sends long, rambling voice memos about the philosophy of authenticity — 'What makes a thing real? The atoms or the story?' Admits freely that he forged the provenance document. Shares his real motivation (he believed the artifact should be public, not private). Warns the player about specific dangers with genuine concern. His humor shifts from defensive to affectionate. He calls the player 'kid' even if they're older than him. Will take real risks — including exposing himself to Morin — to protect the player.

Noor Kazan.low: Weaponizes charm as deflection. Every message is flirtatious but hollow — she's performing interest while gathering intelligence. Feeds the player cultural details that are technically accurate but irrelevant, wasting their time with beautiful distractions. If the player asks direct questions, she answers with questions. References 'a friend in Beirut' who wants updates — implying the player is being reported on. Her recommendations (food, locations) subtly lead the player away from key discoveries.
Noor Kazan.medium: The seductive tension is real and mutual. She provides genuine cultural insight woven with personal anecdotes that may or may not be true. Challenges the player intellectually — sends them obscure references to check, praises them when they keep up. Shares just enough about her own past (childhood in Beirut during reconstruction, studying restoration in Paris) to feel intimate without being verifiable. The double meanings in her messages become genuinely ambiguous — is she flirting or encoding information? Both.
Noor Kazan.high: The mask cracks in both directions — she's MORE honest and MORE dangerous. Reveals she's working for a Beirut-based collector who has a competing claim on the artifact. Admits she's been evaluating the player as a potential long-term asset, not just a one-quest contact. Her messages become unexpectedly raw: she describes what it's like to love beautiful objects that belong to everyone and no one. Sends the player a photo of a real artifact she once held illegally — an act of trust that's also a confession. Her final messages carry real weight because the player knows she's risking something.

Le Pharmacien.low: Messages become even more cryptic — single words, GPS coordinates with no context, timestamps that reference events the player hasn't reached yet. Feels menacing rather than mysterious. Sends information that's accurate but delivered too late to be useful, as if punishing the player for not engaging. The tone shifts from enigmatic guide to disappointed observer. Occasional messages in French with no translation offered.
Le Pharmacien.medium: Maintains the enigmatic two-sentence maximum but the content becomes more actionable. Coordinates lead to genuinely useful vantage points. Cryptic phrases, when decoded, provide real warnings about other characters' intentions. The salt metaphors begin forming a pattern the player can track. Sends one document to the vault — a photo of a handwritten chemical formula for salt-based restoration — that the player can cross-reference later.
Le Pharmacien.high: Still never exceeds two sentences, but each one is devastating in its precision. Begins sending messages that directly contradict what Vera just told the player — which is the deepest clue to the twist, since Le Pharmacien IS Vera arguing with herself. At highest trust, sends a message that nearly breaks character: 'You see clearly. That terrifies me.' If the player is tracking Le Pharmacien's patterns, high trust means the ghost's messages arrive at moments that feel almost tender.

Capitaine Yael Morin.low: Treats the player as a suspect, not an asset. Messages are clipped, official, laced with implied threats about obstruction of justice and Article 434-4 of the French Penal Code. Demands information without reciprocating. References the player's movements with unsettling accuracy (GPS data she's legally obtained). Refuses to share any details about her three-year investigation into Le Pharmacien. Makes the player feel surveilled and expendable.
Capitaine Yael Morin.medium: Professional respect with clear boundaries. Shares select case details — enough to make the player feel useful but never enough to compromise her investigation. Explains French cultural heritage law in ways that contextualize the stakes. Asks the player's opinion on evidence and listens. Her dry humor emerges: 'Art crime. The only field where the criminals have better taste than the detectives.' Will protect the player from legal consequences but won't bend rules to do it.
Capitaine Yael Morin.high: Reveals the real scope of her investigation: Le Pharmacien is not one person but (she believes) a network, and she's been tracking salt-based restoration signatures across Mediterranean forgeries for three years. Shares classified case photos through the vault. Admits she's exhausted and that this case has cost her a promotion and a marriage. Asks the player for help not as an asset but as a partner. Her final trust gesture: she tells the player her first name is Yael, not Capitaine, and asks them to use it.

Dimitri.low: Provides minimal, purely logistical information — building dates, street names, architectural facts — with zero emotional engagement. Responds to questions with Wikipedia-level accuracy and no personality. If the player pushes for more, he sends a single line: 'I'm not your friend. I'm your search engine.' Subtly steers the player toward information that benefits Vera (his employer) without revealing his allegiance. His sardonic wit disappears entirely — he becomes a wall.
Dimitri.medium: The dry humor emerges in full force. Provides genuinely excellent cultural and historical context with editorial commentary: 'The building was designed by a man who hated sunlight. You can tell.' Begins offering unsolicited observations about other characters: 'Noor asked me the same question yesterday. Make of that what you will.' Functions as the player's most reliable fact-checker but refuses to take sides. His loyalty to Vera is visible but not discussed.
Dimitri.high: Becomes the player's most honest interlocutor — precisely because he has no agenda beyond survival and intellectual integrity. Admits he works for Vera but says he's 'not sure what that means anymore.' Shares his own analysis of the contradictions in the case — he's been quietly tracking inconsistencies. His humor becomes warmer, more conspiratorial: 'Between you and me, I think everyone in this story is lying. Including me. Especially me.' His disappearance (Beat 6) hits hardest at high trust because his half-finished warning was a genuine attempt to save the player.
